text: Upon entry and docking, the surgeon begins stripping the peritoneum, ensuring the mesh will sit directly against the fascia. The trainees perform portions of the preperitoneal dissection, carefully avoiding creating holes in the flap.

The surgeon dissects the fat associated with the falciform ligament and peri-umbilical region away from the hernia defect, creating a clear area for mesh placement. The defect is closed transversely using a permanent V-lock suture.

The surgeon performs a straightforward defect closure using a V-lock suture, running it back on itself to secure the closure. Following this, the mesh is positioned over the defect, with a stitch placed in the central portion to aid in positioning. Additional preperitoneal dissection is required to ensure proper mesh placement.

The surgeon ensures the mesh lays flat against the fascia, using absorbable V-lock sutures to fix the mesh by running them from one side to the other. In the background, the needle from the fascial closure is visible, used to tack up the mesh.

The surgeon secures the mesh against the abdominal wall using a circumferential baseball stitch, ensuring it remains stable. The mesh is positioned with some barbs exposed, which is noted as not being a significant concern for adhesions in this procedure.

The surgeon completes circumferential fixation of a ten-centimeter mesh against the abdominal wall, ensuring it lays flat without a central suture line. The mesh is secured using absorbable sutures, demonstrating the final appearance of the mesh fixation.
